title: Validate Binary Search Tree
code: static bool Validate(TreeNode? node, long low, long high)
if (node.val <= low || node.val >= high) return false;

frames.caption: Validate the BST by threading a (low, high) range down from the root, which starts unbounded: (-∞, +∞).
frames.note: Instead of comparing each node to its immediate children, we carry an allowed OPEN interval down the recursion. Every node must fall strictly inside the window its ancestors permit, and the window tightens as we descend — going left lowers the ceiling, going right raises the floor.

frames.caption: Check node 5 against its inherited window: is −∞ < 5 < +∞? Yes → node 5 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Recurse LEFT of 5: tighten the window to (−∞, 5) — every left descendant must stay below 5.
frames.note: Going LEFT, everything in this subtree must stay below the current node, so we tighten the ceiling: high becomes node.val while low is unchanged. This is exactly the rule a parent-only check misses — a deep left descendant must still respect EVERY ancestor it passed, not just its immediate parent.

frames.caption: Check node 3 against its inherited window: is −∞ < 3 < 5? Yes → node 3 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Recurse LEFT of 3: tighten the window to (−∞, 3) — every left descendant must stay below 3.
frames.note: Going LEFT, everything in this subtree must stay below the current node, so we tighten the ceiling: high becomes node.val while low is unchanged. This is exactly the rule a parent-only check misses — a deep left descendant must still respect EVERY ancestor it passed, not just its immediate parent.

frames.caption: Check node 1 against its inherited window: is −∞ < 1 < 3? Yes → node 1 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Recurse RIGHT of 3: tighten the window to (3, 5) — every right descendant must exceed 3.
frames.note: Going RIGHT, everything in this subtree must exceed the current node, so we raise the floor: low becomes node.val while high is unchanged. This is the classic trap in action: node 4 in a tree like 5→8→(4) looks fine next to its parent 8 (4 < 8), but it inherited low = 5 from the root and 4 ≤ 5, so the bounds method correctly rejects it where a child-only comparison would wrongly accept it.

frames.caption: Check node 4 against its inherited window: is 3 < 4 < 5? Yes → node 4 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Recurse RIGHT of 5: tighten the window to (5, +∞) — every right descendant must exceed 5.
frames.note: Going RIGHT, everything in this subtree must exceed the current node, so we raise the floor: low becomes node.val while high is unchanged. This is the classic trap in action: node 4 in a tree like 5→8→(4) looks fine next to its parent 8 (4 < 8), but it inherited low = 5 from the root and 4 ≤ 5, so the bounds method correctly rejects it where a child-only comparison would wrongly accept it.

frames.caption: Check node 8 against its inherited window: is 5 < 8 < +∞? Yes → node 8 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Recurse RIGHT of 8: tighten the window to (8, +∞) — every right descendant must exceed 8.
frames.note: Going RIGHT, everything in this subtree must exceed the current node, so we raise the floor: low becomes node.val while high is unchanged. This is the classic trap in action: node 4 in a tree like 5→8→(4) looks fine next to its parent 8 (4 < 8), but it inherited low = 5 from the root and 4 ≤ 5, so the bounds method correctly rejects it where a child-only comparison would wrongly accept it.

frames.caption: Check node 9 against its inherited window: is 8 < 9 < +∞? Yes → node 9 is in range.
frames.note: The whole method lives or dies on this one line: a node is valid only if its value lies strictly inside the OPEN interval (low, high) it inherited from its ancestors — not merely between its own two children. low and high are the tightest constraints every ancestor has imposed so far. Bounds are long (not int) so a node legitimately holding int.MinValue/int.MaxValue still passes.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Reached a null link → an empty subtree is trivially a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: A null link is the base case: an empty subtree imposes no constraint and is trivially a valid BST, so we return true and let the && upstream keep folding the children's results together.

frames.caption: Every node satisfied its (low, high) bounds → the tree IS a valid BST → return true.
frames.note: Every node passed its (low, high) test, so the && chain folds all the way up to true. Each node is checked exactly once against bounds inherited in O(1), giving O(n) time and O(h) recursion-stack space. The equivalent check is that an inorder walk is strictly increasing — both encode the same BST invariant.
